associations.item1_title: AI-Extracted Eye-Tracking Features for ADHD Screening (Chen 2023)
associations.item2_title: Unraveling ADHD Through Eye Tracking: Pupil Metrics (2023)
associations.description: AI-extracted eye-tracking features show 89% sensitivity for ADHD screening, while raw pupil metrics show only 57% sensitivity, with conflicting conclusions about clinical utility.
associations.significance: Core methodological debate in ADHD eye-tracking field.

associations.item2_title: Reading Eye Movements in ADHD Children (2023)
associations.description: CPT-based eye-tracking and naturalistic reading eye-tracking provide complementary perspectives on attentional dysfunction in ADHD—structured task performance vs. real-world reading behavior.
associations.significance: Suggests that eye-tracking measures across different task contexts may provide complementary diagnostic information.

associations.item1_title: Dual System Cognitive Enhancement with VR and Eye-Tracking Training for ADHD (2024)
associations.item2_title: AI-Extracted Eye-Tracking Features for ADHD Screening (Chen 2023)
associations.description: The 2024 VR+eye-tracking intervention study builds on the screening findings from Chen 2023 by using eye-tracking as both an assessment and training modality, representing an application evolution from diagnosis to intervention.
associations.significance: Shows translational potential of eye-tracking biomarkers beyond screening into intervention design.

research_gaps.description: No study has investigated whether eye-tracking features that are discriminative for ADHD screening (identified in cross-sectional studies) can serve as predictive biomarkers for treatment response to eye-tracking-based interventions (such as the VR+eye-tracking training paradigm).
research_gaps.evidence: assoc_003 connects screening research (Chen 2023) with intervention research (Dual System VR 2024), but no study bridges these by testing whether screening biomarkers predict training outcomes.

research_gaps.description: Existing ADHD eye-tracking studies focus predominantly on school-aged children (ages 6–14). No studies have validated eye-tracking biomarkers or training protocols in adult ADHD populations, adolescents (15–18), or preschool-aged children (3–5), leaving the developmental trajectory of eye-tracking biomarkers unknown.
research_gaps.evidence: All five studies cited across associations focus on children aged 6–14. Adult ADHD populations (prevalence ~2.5%) are entirely unrepresented in the eye-tracking ADHD literature sampled.
